text.title: Load Balancing Algorithms Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the lb-algorithms methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Round-robin (or weighted round-robin) MUST be chosen only when ALL backend servers have equal capacity AND request processing time is uniform (p99 / p50 ≤ 1.2). For any workload with variable-cost endpoints (mixed fast/slow API, large file uploads alongside short reads), select least-connections instead.
rule.rationale: Round-robin distributes by connection count, not CPU cost. A server handling one long-running request alongside many short ones accumulates queue depth while still receiving equal new connections — uneven utilisation that round-robin cannot self-correct.

rule.statement: Do NOT use IP hash in autoscaling environments OR behind NAT / VPN. If session persistence is required, use cookie-based sticky sessions instead.
rule.rationale: IP hash routes all traffic from a client IP to one backend; when backends scale in/out the sticky property breaks. NAT collapses thousands of real users to one IP → one backend gets a thundering herd. Cookie persistence handles both correctly.

rule.statement: For any workload with variable processing time (WebSocket connections, database proxies, long-running HTTP uploads/downloads, streaming endpoints, mixed fast/slow API routes), choose `leastconn` (or `weighted leastconn` for heterogeneous capacity) as the default algorithm.
rule.rationale: Least-connections adapts to request cost naturally — a backend processing a slow request accumulates connection count and receives fewer new ones. The per-connection tracking overhead is negligible compared to the queueing it prevents.

rule.statement: When fleet capacity is heterogeneous (servers with different CPU/RAM), set connection weights proportional to actual capacity (e.g., 100 / 100 / 50 for two large + one small). Plain (unweighted) algorithms MUST NOT be used on heterogeneous fleets.
rule.rationale: Without weights, small servers receive equal load and saturate first; the cluster behaves as if its capacity equals N × the smallest server. Weights restore proportional distribution.

rule.statement: Before picking an algorithm for a new LB, measure (or estimate from APM) the actual backend response-time distribution. If p99 and p50 differ by more than 2×, default to least-connections; if within ±20%, round-robin is acceptable.
rule.rationale: Algorithm decisions made without data produce subtle load imbalances that are invisible until a backend falls behind and starts dropping under peak. Measurement first eliminates guesswork.
